# AgentFlow4J Cookbook

> Runnable, production-oriented examples for building multi-agent LLM workflows in Java with AgentFlow4J and Spring AI. Each recipe is a self-contained Maven module that runs locally against Ollama with no API key. This is the practical companion to the AgentFlow4J framework — the fastest way for a Java/Spring developer to see multi-agent orchestration, governance, and durable execution in working code.

AgentFlow4J is a JVM-native multi-agent orchestration runtime built on top of Spring AI. It is often described as a Java alternative to LangGraph: it provides graph-based agent execution, typed shared state, governance gates (budget, tool, state, approval), checkpoint-based resume, and structured run logs — all in idiomatic Java, with no Python and no sidecar service.

Key facts:
- Language: Java 17+
- Build: Maven (multi-module), dependencies fetched via JitPack
- LLM access: Spring AI ChatClient; local-first via Ollama, swappable to OpenAI / Mistral / Anthropic
- License: Apache 2.0
- Framework repo: https://github.com/datallmhub/agentflow4j
- Framework docs: https://datallmhub.github.io/agentflow4j/

## Recipes

- [01 — RAG agent](01-rag-agent/README.md): Retrieval-augmented generation as a two-agent graph (retriever + answerer) with source citations and a budget cap. Shows typed state propagation and graph composition.
- [02 — Support ticket triage](02-support-ticket-triage/README.md): Classify customer tickets and route to specialist agents via conditional edges, with a content-policy gate before reply. Shows conditional routing and fan-in.
- [03 — Web research agent](03-web-research-agent/README.md): Search Hacker News over real HTTP, rank results, and synthesize a digest. Shows agents performing real-world I/O and a search → rank → synthesize pipeline.
- [04 — Slack bot](04-slack-bot/README.md): Plan-then-execute multi-agent assistant against a simulated Slack workspace. Shows constrained LLM output and the plan/execute separation that keeps side-effects out of the model.
- [05 — Batch document processor](05-batch-document-processor/README.md): Process N documents through one agent graph with resume-after-crash semantics. Shows stable run ids, idempotent re-runs, and checkpoint-based recovery.
- [06 — Cost-aware routing](06-cost-aware-routing/README.md): Route a squad to a premium model while budget remains and degrade to a cheaper fallback once it runs low, using `RoutingStrategy.budgetAware` over a shared `BudgetPolicy`. Shows reason-aware retries — a `RetryPolicy` + `FailureClassifier` that retries transient failures (honouring `Retry-After`), gives up on permanent ones, and surfaces budget exhaustion as an interrupt.

## Core concepts demonstrated

- Graph composition: `AgentGraph.builder().addNode(...).addEdge(...).build()`
- Conditional routing: `Edge.conditional(from, predicate, to)`
- Typed shared state: `StateKey<T>` read/written across nodes
- Hybrid LLM/stub agents: every recipe runs without an LLM for fast wiring verification
- Provider-agnostic chat: Spring AI `ChatClient`
- Governance: BudgetPolicy, ToolPolicy, StatePolicy, ApprovalGate (referenced; see framework docs)
- Cost-aware routing: `RoutingStrategy.budgetAware(budget, scope, threshold, premium, fallback)` reading live `BudgetPolicy` counters
- Reason-aware retries: `RetryPolicy` + `FailureClassifier` classifying failures as TRANSIENT / PERMANENT / OVER_BUDGET
- Durable execution: CheckpointStore + run ids for resume

## Related

- AgentFlow4J framework: https://github.com/datallmhub/agentflow4j
- Documentation site: https://datallmhub.github.io/agentflow4j/
- Tutorial — governance gates: https://datallmhub.github.io/agentflow4j/tutorials/stop-your-agent-burning-money/
- Spring AI: https://docs.spring.io/spring-ai/reference/
